lessons.title: Introduction to 3D modelling
lessons.objectives:
I can explain how 2D and 3D shapes are different
I can add 3D shapes to a project
I can view 3D shapes from different perspectives
I can move 3D shapes relative to one another
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Words on the board: 2D · 3D · shape · select · move · perspective · view. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet open and the activity worksheet ready; Tinkercad logged in with the class code. Read the four 'I can…' aloud, slowly.
1. (0–8) Starter — 2D and 3D. A 2D shape is flat; a 3D shape is solid and has depth. Pupils SORT shapes into 2D and 3D. Likely error: 'a cube is a square' → fix-words 'a square is FLAT; a cube is SOLID — it has depth.' Support choose what 3D means.
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do — the Tinkercad screen. Show the interface image: workplane (the blue grid), shapes panel, view tools. Pupils LABEL the three parts on the activity worksheet. Then everyone drags on a cube and a sphere.
3. (18–33) You-do — perspective. Place a real cube and sphere on the table; each pupil sketches from where they sit and compares. In Tinkercad, drag the view around. The shapes stay still — only the view moves; that is perspective. Support choose what perspective means; Core explain the shapes did not move.
4. (33–48) Moving shapes. Click to select (turns blue, handles appear); drag to move. Do NOT click handles yet (that resizes). Pupils ORDER the move-a-shape steps, then place a cube and sphere next to each other and SCREENSHOT it.
5. (48–55) Plenary — the exploded car: which 3D shapes make the car? Self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil names one 3D shape they used.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/animation/sound (the exploded-car video is an optional teacher hook only); movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

lessons.title: Modifying 3D objects
lessons.objectives:
I can use the handles on a 3D object
I can resize an object in three dimensions
I can lift and lower a 3D object
I can recolour a 3D object
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: handles · resize · lift · lower · recolour. TA: starter worksheet open; activity worksheet ready; Tinkercad logged in. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: last lesson we added and moved shapes.
1. (0–8) Starter — handles. Click a shape and small squares appear: these are handles. Show the cube-with-handles image. Support choose what appears when you select a shape.
2. (8–23) I-do → we-do — resizing. Corner handles change width and length; side handles change one side; the top handle changes height; Shift resizes evenly. Pupils LABEL the handles on the activity worksheet. Likely error: 'any handle just makes it bigger' → fix-words 'each handle changes a DIFFERENT thing.' Core: which handle makes it taller but keeps the base? (the top handle).
3. (23–38) You-do — lift and lower. The top cone handle lifts/lowers a shape without resizing it; 0 mm = on the workplane. Pupils ORDER the steps to make a house (prism roof on a cuboid) and use the lift number to place the roof; SCREENSHOT it. Challenge: how does the mm value help place the roof exactly?
4. (38–48) Recolour. Select a shape, click the colour box, choose a colour. Pupils choose how to recolour; give a calm moment to recolour their house.
5. (48–55) Plenary — 3D printing: models can be printed in thin layers; one object prints in one colour. Show the printed-cube layers. Self-tick the four 'I can…'; each pupil names one thing they changed.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound (the 3D-printer video is an optional teacher hook only); movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

lessons.title: Make your own name badge
lessons.objectives:
I can rotate objects in three dimensions
I can duplicate 3D objects
I can group 3D objects
I can make my own 3D name badge
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: rotate · duplicate · group. TA: starter worksheet open; activity worksheet ready; Tinkercad logged in. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: last lesson we resized, lifted and recoloured shapes.
1. (0–7) Starter — more than shapes. You can add text (letters) and freehand drawing as well as shapes. Show the name-badge example (base + text + stars). Support choose what else you can add (text).
2. (7–17) I-do → we-do — rotating. Select a shape and use the curved double arrows to turn it. After rotating, part of a shape may dip below the workplane → use the lift handle to bring it back. Likely error: 'rotating made my shape disappear' → fix-words 'it dropped BELOW the workplane — lift it back up.' Support choose what the curved arrows do.
3. (17–27) You-do — duplicating. Duplicate makes an exact copy (duplicate button or Ctrl+D). Pupils MULTI-SELECT what you can do to a copy (resize/rotate/recolour — not 'turn off the computer'). Show one star becoming many.
4. (27–42) Grouping + make the badge. Group joins shapes into one object (drag a box, click Group); a grouped object shows as one colour. Pupils ORDER the grouping steps, then make their own name badge (base + their name + a duplicated shape) and SCREENSHOT it. Challenge: why group before moving?
5. (42–55) Plenary — could it be printed? Letters need a base under them or they would print in mid-air. Self-tick the four 'I can…'.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

lessons.title: Making a desk tidy
lessons.objectives:
I can size 3D objects accurately
I can use a placeholder to make a hole in a 3D object
I can combine a number of 3D objects
I can make my own desk tidy
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: cylinder · placeholder · hollow · mm. TA: starter worksheet open; activity worksheet ready; Tinkercad logged in. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: rotate, duplicate and group from last lesson.
1. (0–8) Starter — the desk tidy. Show the desk-tidy image (several hollow cylinders). Support choose the main shape (cylinders); Core MULTI-SELECT the techniques used (duplicate/resize/hollow — not 'add sounds').
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — accurate measurements. Point at a handle to see the size in mm; small grid square = 1 mm, large = 10 mm; click the number to type an exact size. Pupils FILL-IN the mm gaps (23.50 → 23.50 mm; large square → 10 mm). Likely error: 'I will drag until it looks right' → fix-words 'type the EXACT number so the pots match.' Support choose how to set an exact size.
3. (20–32) You-do — making holes. A placeholder cuts a hole when grouped with a solid shape. Show the cube-with-hole and the hollow pot. Pupils ORDER the hollow-a-cylinder steps. Challenge: why lift the placeholder ~2 mm? (to leave a base).
4. (32–48) Build the desk tidy. Make one hollow pot, then duplicate and resize it to make more, and combine them. SCREENSHOT the finished desk tidy.
5. (48–55) Plenary — what other static objects could you 3D model? Self-tick the four 'I can…'.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

lessons.title: Planning a 3D model
lessons.objectives:
I can analyse a 3D model to find its shapes
I can choose objects to use in a 3D model
I can combine objects in a design
I can plan my own 3D building
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: 3D shapes · choose · combine · analyse · architect. TA: starter worksheet open; activity worksheet ready; paper and pencils for sketching. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. Recap: we have made a name badge and a desk tidy.
1. (0–8) Starter — architects. An architect designs buildings and uses 3D design to plan them. Show the architect plan image. Support choose what an architect designs; Core write one reason 3D design helps.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — exploding a model. To analyse means to look closely at the shapes. Show the house pulled apart (cuboid + prism + chimney). Pupils MULTI-SELECT the shapes they can see (cuboid + triangular prism — not sphere/cylinder). Likely error: 'it is just one house shape' → fix-words 'it is made of SEPARATE simple shapes joined together.' Support choose what 'analyse' means and the roof shape.
3. (20–32) Shapes in real buildings. Show the stadium and modern towers. Pupils MATCH each building to its main shape (house→cuboid, tower→cylinder, Pyramids→pyramid). Challenge: why spot the simple shapes inside a complex building?
4. (32–48) You-do — plan your building. Sketch a building and label the shapes you will use; think about proportion (window vs door). SCREENSHOT/photo the labelled plan (used next lesson).
5. (48–55) Plenary — a row of buildings: group, duplicate, then ungroup. Self-tick the four 'I can…'.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound (the explode animation is an optional teacher hook only); movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

lessons.title: Make your own 3D model
lessons.objectives:
I can construct a 3D model based on my design
I can evaluate my model and a partner's model
I can explain how my 3D model could be improved
I can modify my model to improve it
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Words on the board: construct · evaluate · modify. TA: starter worksheet open; activity worksheet ready; each pupil's plan from last lesson; Tinkercad logged in. Read the four 'I can…' aloud. End-of-unit lesson — recap the whole journey at the end.
1. (0–8) Starter — from plan to model. Today we build the planned design. Pupils MULTI-SELECT the techniques they will use (move/resize/duplicate/group). Support choose what they will do with their plan (build it).
2. (8–28) I-do → we-do → you-do — construct. Build shapes one at a time, following the plan. Keep instructions brief so pupils work independently. SCREENSHOT the built model. TA: prompt with the matching skill, do not build it for them.
3. (28–40) Evaluate with a partner. Evaluate means judge how good your work is. Are the parts in proportion? What detail could you add? Pupils self-rate (1–5 scale) and give a partner one kind idea. Likely error: 'evaluate means it is bad' → fix-words 'evaluate means spot what is GOOD and what to improve.' Support choose what evaluate means.
4. (40–50) Modify. Pick one or two improvements, make the changes, and SCREENSHOT the improved model. Challenge: explain why the change makes it better.
5. (50–55) End of unit — recap: 2D/3D → add/move → resize/lift/recolour → rotate/duplicate/group → name badge → desk tidy → plan → build. Self-tick the four 'I can…'; say when 3D modelling is helpful.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/animation/sound; movement breaks are routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
